channel.title: Notes
channel.generator: Webpage HTML Export plugin for Obsidian

item.title: Index
item.description: This exists because I couldn't get Obsidian to sync nicely on my phone. Some figures are not included.

item.title: Sequences
item.description:
Definition 1 (Convergence). A sequence of real numbers converges to the real number if for all there exists such that for all we have . Definition 2 (Monotonic). A sequence is called decreasing if and increasing if for all . A sequence that is increasing or decreasing is monotonic. Theorem 3. All bounded monotone sequences converge Proof. Without loss of generality, assume is increasing. Let . Observe that for all , cannot be an upper bound, so there exists such that . Since is increasing, for all . It follows that for all , so . □Theorem 4. If is unbounded and increasing, then Proof. Let . Since is unbounded, there exists such that . Since is increasing, for all . □Definition 5 ( and ). Let be a sequence and denote and . We define and as follows: This offers a new perspective from which to understand convergence. We no longer require that we know what the sequence converges to ahead of time to test its convergence.Theorem 6. exists if and only if . Proof. Assume exists We consider two cases: Case 1: . Suppose and let be a positive integer. Then, there exists a positive integer such that for all , . Note that so . Similar logic holds for the case.Case 2: is finite. Our goal is to bound and and squeeze them onto . Suppose where is a real number and let . We know that there must exist such that for all . We can rewrite this as for all , soThus, for all , we have . Taking and applying the definition of , we have . Since can be arbitrarily small, we have shown that . Applying the same logic to and bounding it below by , we can show that , from which we conclude that as can be arbitrarily small.Because and we have shown that , we conclude that .Assume We seek to prove the existence of exists. Once again, we consider two cases: Case 1: . This case is quite obvious. Suppose . This means that for all , there exists some such that . Since for all , it follows that for all . By definition, we have . We can apply the same logic using and the .Case 2: are finite. Suppose , where . We seek to prove that . Let . Like in the other direction, we will do this by bounding around mk. Since , there exists a positive integer such that ^058ff7 This means that , which implies for all . Similarly, since , there exists a positive integer such that which means or for all . Together, we have for all . Therefore, . □Definition 7 (Cauchy Sequence). A sequence of real numbers is Cauchy if for all , there exists such that for any , we have . This gives an alternative, yet (as we will show) equivalent, perspective to study the convergence of sequences without knowing what a sequence converges to ahead of time.Lemma 8. Convergent sequences are Cauchy. Proof. Let and suppose converges to . By definition, there exist such that for all , Note that . Hence, is Cauchy. □Lemma 9. Cauchy sequences are bounded. Proof. Suppose is Cauchy. This means that there exists an such that for all , we have . Since , we can fix , giving for all . Since , it follows that for all , so we can bound all the terms after . Since is a finite number, we can bound all the terms before by . Hence, all of the terms in the sequence can be bounded by . □Theorem 10. A sequence is convergent if and only if it is Cauchy. Proof. Conveniently, we have already shown in a Lemma that convergent sequences are Cauchy. We now prove that Cauchy sequences are convergent.Suppose is a Cauchy sequence. This means that for all , there exists such that for all , we have . We rewrite this asTaking , we find that . But we know that . Thus, we have shown that , so converges. □Remark 11. The Completeness Axiom plays an important role in this proof, as it assures us that and are meaningful here. Definition 12 (Subsequence). Suppose is a sequence. Given a sequence in , denoted as , is subsequence of . The following theorem is particularly useful in proving the existence of a subsequence if its limit is known.Theorem 13. For all , there exists a monotonic subsequence of converging to if and only if the set is infinite for all . Proof. The forward direction is fairly obvious and follows from the definition of convergence. We construct a subsequence as follows. Pick any . Let . Since is infinite for all , the set is infinite. We define the th element of our constructed sequence as such that and . Note that the latter condition (which ensures that we construct a valid subsequence) can always be satisfied since we have infinitely many choices and only finitely many invalid choices. \ref{}By ^f90a72Theorem 13, we can select a monotonic subsequence. Without loss of generality, suppose is selected to be monotonic.We must now show that actually converges to . By the Archimedean property, we know that for all , there exists such that . Let . Then, for all , we have . □Theorem 14. If a sequence is unbounded above/below, it has a subsequence with limit . Proof. Observe that if and the sequence is unbounded, we can easily construct a subsequence with limit by repeatedly choosing more extreme values, which are guaranteed to exist due to the sequence being unbounded. □Remark 15. An interesting related result is that all real numbers can be expressed as the limit of some subsequence of the rationals. Theorem 16. If converges, then every subsequence converges to the same limit. Proof. Let and . Since converges, there exists such that for all , . Let be a subsequence of . We observe that for all , which is easily proved via induction. Take such that . It follows that for all , we have , so . Thus, . □Theorem 17. Every sequence has a monotone subsequence. Proof. We define to be dominant if for all . Consider the following cases:Case 1: There are infinitely many dominant terms. Let be a dominant term. Since , we have . Thus, we can easily construct a decreasing subsequence.Case 2: There are finitely many dominant terms. There exists such that is not dominant for all . Pick . It follows that is not dominant, so there exists such that . We know that is not dominant as . We repeat this process to form an increasing subsequence. Hence, we can always construct a monotone subsequence. □Theorem 18 (Bolzano-Weierstrass). Every bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence. Proof.
By ^5c8e8fTheorem 17, every sequence has a monotone subsequence. If we have a bounded sequence, it must also have a bounded monotone subsequence, which, by ^f13358Theorem 3, converge. There is another less elegant proof involving interval division resulting in a Cauchy sequence. □Remark 19. Interestingly, several of the statements we have proved are equivalent to the Completeness Axiom. In particular, the following are equivalent: Completeness of
Cauchy sequences convergeCauchy sequences converge
Bounded monotonic sequences convergeBounded monotonic sequences converge
Bolzano-Weierstrass TheoremBolzano-Weierstrass Theorem Definition 20 (Subsequential Limit). Let be a sequence in . A subsequential limit is any real number or symbol or that is the limit of some subsequence of . Theorem 21. Let be a sequence. There exists a monotone subsequence whose limit is and a monotone subsequence whose limit is . Proof.
We have shownWe have shown that if is unbounded, then there exists a subsequence whose limit is . Assume that is bounded. We have . Using our notationnotation, we have Let . From the limit, we know that there exists such that for all , or Suppose for contradiction that holds for only finitely many . Then, there exists such that for all , . This implies two possibilities: Suppose . This implies , which is a contradiction of . Suppose . This implies , which is a contradiction of .
Hence, the is infinite. It followsIt follows that there exists a monotonicmonotonic subsequence that converges to . A similar proof holds for . □ Theorem 22. Let be any sequence in and let denote the set of subsequential limits of . Then, (i) is nonempty. (ii) and . (iii) exists if and only if has exactly one element. Proof.
(i) follows from the factthe fact that there exist subsequences with limit and , which are guaranteed to exist.
We now prove (ii). Suppose is unbounded from above. ThenThen, there exists a subsequence such that , so . A similar proof holds for sequences unbounded from below regarding and .
Now, assume is bounded. Take and assume . ThenThen, , where the subscript appended to and indicate that their limits are taken with respect to . Note that for all . We haveSince can be any element in , we have
We have provedproved that previously. Thus, and . Therefore, and .
Part (iii) is simply a restatement of ^290651Theorem 6. □Theorem 23. Suppose is a sequence in and . Then, . Remark 24. This result shows that is closed, but is not particularly interesting. Its proof is left as an exercise for the overly-motivated reader. Remark 25 (Recall). Let be a sequence and denote and . We define and as follows: Theorem 26. If converges to a positive number and is any sequence, then . Proof. We first seek to showWe have 3 cases:Case 1: is finite.
We have shownshown that there must exist a subsequence such that . Furthermore, since all subsequences of a convergent sequence have the same limit, we havehave , so . Since is the largest possible limit of a sequence , it follows that . Case 2: .
Since is unbounded, there existsthere exists a subsequence of such that . Since , . Hence, .Case 3: . Since , . Clearly, .Thus, in all cases. Note that for finitely many terms as exists (otherwise we would have a subsequential limit of , which implies or the limit doesn't exist), so we can omit these finite terms. Noting that , we replace with and with in the inequality we just proved, givingyieldingHence, . □Remark 27 (Useful facts). If , then for every , the set is finite. If , then for every , the set is infinite. The set can be infinite. For example, take . Definition 28 (Series). is a partial sum of . We say that the infinite series converges if the sequence of partial sums converges to a real number . A series that does not converge is said to diverge. Definition 29 (Absolute and Conditional Convergence). A series is said to be absolutely convergent if converges. Otherwise, it is conditionally convergent. Definition 30 (Cauchy Criterion). A series satisfies the Cauchy criterion if its sequence of partial sums is a Cauchy sequence. Equivalently, for all , there must exist such that for all Theorem 31. A series converges if and only if it satisfies the Cauchy criterion. Proof.
The sequence of partial sums converges if and only if the series converges. For the sequence of partial sums to converge, it must satisfy the Cauchy CriterionCauchy Criterion. □Theorem 32. If converges, . Proof. Take in the Cauchy criterion. □Theorem 33 (Comparison Test). Let be a series where for all . If converges and for all , then converges. If diverges and for all , then diverges. Proof. Suppose . Then,
by the Triangle InequalityTriangle Inequality. Since converges, it must satisfy the Cauchy CriterionCauchy Criterion. It follows that must also satisfy the Cauchy CriterionCauchy Criterion, so it converges.The divergent case is trivial. □Theorem 34 (Ratio Test). A series of nonzero terms converges absolutely if diverges if otherwise, no useful information is acquired Proof. Suppose . Choose such that . Then, there exists an such that so for all . Note that soThis forms a convergent geometric series.The divergent case follows the same logic. □Theorem 35 (Root Test). Let be a series and let . The series converges absolutely if diverges if otherwise no useful information is acquired Proof.
Suppose . Choose such that . Then, there exists an such that , so for all . Since , the geometric series converges. By ^9566f0Theorem 33, also converges.
If , then ^82f457Theorem 21 tells us that a subsequence of as limit , so for infinitely many . Thus, , so must diverge. □ The Root test is a stronger test. If it gives no information, the Ratio test has no hope of being useful either.Remark 36. We can see both the Ratio and Root tests fail to give information using the series and . Theorem 37 (Alternating Series). If and , then the alternating series converges. Moreover, the partial sums satisfy for all . Proof. Consider the subsequences and of . Note that is increasing since . Also, is decreasing as . We can writeso is bounded above by . We can also write
so is bounded below by . Hence, both subsequences are bounded and monotone, so by ^f13358Theorem 3 they must converge. Let and . Then, , so . Hence, .Additionally, note that so . Hence, . □Hypothesis 38. Rearranging terms in an infinite sum can change the result. For absolutely converging series, rearranging the terms in the summation does not affect the sum. However, things are bit more interesting when it comes to conditionally converging series.Theorem 39 (Riemann's Rearrangement Theorem). Suppose converges conditionally. By rearranging terms, the resultant sum can be made to converge to any real number or to diverge. Proof. Defineand Since converges conditionally, both and diverge.For all , let be the smallest positive integer such that . Let be the smallest positive integer such that . We continue this process, obtaining values for and alternately, to obtain a sequence . Let be the partial sum of this sequence. For any , we have for some . Observe that

item.title: Continuity
item.description:
Definition 1 ( and ). The set on which a function is defined is . The set of values of is . Definition 2 (Continuous Function at a Point). We say is continuous at a point if , such that , Definition 3 (Continuous Function at a Point). A function is continuous at if for every sequence such that , we have . Conversely, is not continuous if there exists a sequence such that but does not converge. We claim that these 2 definitions are equally valid ways of approaching continuity. It is, however, frequently more convenient to work with ^7798d4Definition 3 as it allows us to take advantage of limit properties we have already proven.
Theorem 4. ^4b09efDefinition 2 and ^7798d4Definition 3 are equivalent. Proof.
Suppose that a function is continuous according to ^4b09efDefinition 2. Consider a sequence in that converges to . Let . By ^4b09efDefinition 2, there exists such that for all , if , then . Furthermore, as converges to , there exists such that for all , we have . By ^4b09efDefinition 2, it follows that for all , we have . Hence, , so ^7798d4Definition 3 holds.
Conversely, suppose is continuous according to ^7798d4Definition 3. Suppose for contradiction that ^4b09efDefinition 2 does not hold. Then, there exists such that for all and for all that satisfy ,
Fix . Consider a sequence such that for all . Note that this sequence converges to . However, . This contradicts ^7798d4Definition 3. □Example 5. Show at is continuous.
Choose . For all , we have by the Triangle InequalityTriangle Inequality. Then, .Theorem 6. If is continuous at , the and for all are continuous at . Furthermore, if is continuous at , so are , , and if . Proof.
Consider a sequence such that . Since is continuous, by ^7798d4Definition 3 we have . By limit properties, we have . Once again, by ^7798d4Definition 3, we have that is continuous at . We can shamelessly apply the properties of limits to prove the other statements in a similar manner.
Let . Since is continuous, by ^4b09efDefinition 2, there exists such that for all such that , we have . Then, by the Reverse Triangle InequalityReverse Triangle Inequality, so is continuous at . □Theorem 7. If is continuous at and is continuous at , then is continuous at . Proof. Consider a sequence that converges to . Since is continuous at , . Since is continuous at , . □ This result is particularly important as it allows us to examine the continuity of a function using functions we already know are continuous.Example 8. If and are continuous, then is continuous.
Observe that . We know that , , and are continuous, so after applying ^521a51Theorem 6, we find that the combined expression is also continuous. Definition 9 (Continuous Function on a Set). A function is continuous on a set if is continuous at every . Definition 10 (Continuous Function). is a continuous function of is continuous at all .
Definition 11 (Bounded Function). A real-valued function is bounded if is boundedbounded. Theorem 12. Let be a continuous real-valued function on a closed interval . Then, the minimum and maximum values exist and can be achieved by . Consequently, is bounded on . Proof.
Let . Suppose for contradiction that is unbounded on , so . We construct a sequence such that . By Bolzano-WeierstrassBolzano-Weierstrass, there is a subsequence of that converges to some real number . Since is closed, . Since is continuous, . However, since , we have that by construction. This is a contradiction. Thus, .
Since is not an upper bound, there exists a sequence such that , so . By Bolzano-WeierstrassBolzano-Weierstrass, there exists a subsequence of that converges to some real number . Since is continuous, . By Sequences > ^f8831eTheorem 16, since converges, we have . Therefore, . □
Remark 13. Note that we cannot be sure that converges purely by its construction, so we employed Bolzano-WeierstrassBolzano-Weierstrass to arrive at a limit. Furthermore, Sequences > ^f90a72Theorem 13 is false on open intervals. Theorem 14 (Intermediate Value Theorem). If is a continuous real-valued function on an interval and or for any such that , there exists an such that . Proof. Without loss of generality, assume . Define . Let . Note that . We first seek to establish a lower bound on and construct a sequence that converges to from below. Since is not an upper bound, there exists an such that . Then, , so by the continuity of . Since for all , we have .Now we establish an upper bound on and construct a sequence that converges to from above. Let . It follows that , so . Since , we have and . Hence, for all so . Therefore, . □Corollary 15. If is a continuous real-valued function on an interval , then the set is also an interval or a single point. Proof.
Let . By the Intermediate Value Theoremthe Intermediate Value Theorem, for all such that , we have . Suppose . Then there exist such that . Thus, , so is an interval with endpoints and . If , then . □Theorem 16. Let be a strictly increasing function on an interval such that is an interval . Then is continuous on . Proof. Suppose . Assume for simplicity that is not an endpoint of , so there exists such that . Let and assume . Then, there exist such that and . Since is strictly increasing, we have . Choose . If , then soHence, is continuous. □Definition 17 (Fixed Point). Let be a continuous function. We say is a fixed point if . Example 18. Let be a continuous function from to . Prove that always has a fixed point.
The geometric intuition here is quite nice. Note that must cross the diagonal of the unit square formed by the domain and codomain. Define and note that if and only if is a fixed point. Furthermore, but . By |the Intermediate Value Theorem|the Intermediate Value Theorem, there exists an such that . Hence, a fixed point always exists.Definition 19 (Uniformly Continuous). Let be a real-valued function defined on a set . Then is uniformly continuous on if for all , there exists such that for all such that , we have .
The concept of uniform continuity is a stronger statement than ^4b09efDefinition 2. Whereas in ^4b09efDefinition 2 we could have depend on , here we have no information about where we are in the domain. Hence, our choice of may only depend on .Theorem 20. If is continuous on a closed interval , then is uniformly continuous on . Proof.
Suppose for contradiction that is not uniformly continuous on . Then, for all , there exist such that yet . By Bolzano-WeierstrassBolzano-Weierstrass, we have a subsequence of that converges to . Since is closed, . Note that . Since is continuous at , we haveso which contradicts our assumption that for all . □
Remark 21. ^c27c6aTheorem 20 only requires that the set is bounded and closed.

item.title: Numbers
item.description:
Definition 1 (Positive Integers). We denote the set of positive integers by . Axiom 2 (Peano Axioms). 1 belongs to If , then its successor belongs to 1 is not a successor of any element in If and have the same successor, then A subset of which contains 1 and contains whenever it contains must equal The ^28570aAxiom 2 offer a rigorous construction of . It also forms the foundation for mathematical induction.Note that operations like subtraction allow us to escape . We extend our exploration beyond :Definition 3 (Integers). We define the set of integers to be , where . Note that is closed under addition, subtraction, and multiplication. Still, we are able to escape through basic arithmetic, namely division. We attempt to fill in this gap:Definition 4 (Rational Numbers). We define the set of rational numbers to be under the equivalence relation where . In an equivalence class, there is a unique representative where are coprime and . We remain unsatisfied as there are still ways we can escape our new set . We define the following to help understand why:Definition 5 (Algebraic Number). A number is called an algebraic number if it satisfies a polynomial equation where , , and . Note that not all algebraic numbers are rational (e.g. ). Naturally, we would like to be able to tell when an algebraic number is rational and when it is not.Theorem 6 (Rational Zeros Theorem). Suppose , and is a solution to Let where and are coprime and . Then, and . Proof. We haveso . Since are coprime, we conclude that .Similarly, we haveso .□Corollary 7. Consider where . Any rational solution is integer. Proof.
Suppose is a solution, where are coprime and . By the ^d8a847Theorem 6, so . Thus, . □
Remark 8. The ^d8a847Theorem 6 is incredibly useful for proving that a given algebraic number is irrational. We do so by constructing the polynomial equation that it is a root of and then apply the theorem to show that it has no rational solutions. Remark 9. and are ordered fields, which give them very nice properties. We examine some basic properties of .Definition 10 (Absolute Value). We define if and if . Definition 11. For numbers and , we define . The absolute value has some useful properties, including for all
The ^28152a^28152a Theorem 12 (Triangle Inequality). For all , we have and We define some concepts to help examine the structure of a set.Definition 13 (Maximum and Minimum). Let be a nonempty subset of . if and for all . if and for all . Remark 14. If is finite, and are guaranteed to exist. Definition 15 (Upper and Lower Bound). Let be a nonempty subset of . If such that for all , is an upper bound of , and is bounded above. If such that for all , is an lower bound of , and is bounded below. If is bounded above and bounded below, it is bounded. Definition 16 (Supremum and Infimum). Let be a nonempty subset of . The supremum of , , is the least upper bound of if it exists. The infimum of , , is the least greatest bound of if it exists. Now we are ready to discuss the Completeness Axiom.Axiom 17 (Completeness Axiom). Every nonempty subset that is bounded above has a least upper bound. Theorem 18 (Archimedian Property). If and , then for some positive , we have . Proof.
Suppose for contradiction that there exist and such that for all . This means that the set is bounded above by . By the ^02907aAxiom 17, it follows that exists. Since is the least upper bound, is not an upper bound, so there exists such that . Rearranging, we have , so is not an upper bound. This is a contradiction. □Theorem 19 (Denseness of ). If and , then there exists such that . Proof. We want to show that there exist such that or
Since , the ^379052Theorem 18 ensures that there exists such that Now we must show that there exists an such that . Note that there exists such that . Consider the set , which contains the integers bounded by . Also, consider the set . Note that these sets are finite and nonempty (since they contain ). Fix , so is the least integer greater than . We haveSince , , so . Hence, . Thus, . □Although and are not real numbers, they are extremely useful when it comes to studying the behavior of real numbers. For example, for a set , we write if is unbounded above. Like how we developed from and from , we can attempt to construct from . In particular, we consider the Dedekind cut.We begin by observing that for all . Definition 20 (Dedekind Cut). If for some we have such that and is not empty If , , and , then contains no largest rational then is a Dedekind cut. Furthermore, . We can define the set to be the space of Dedekind cuts. Arithmetic operators and ordering properties on can be defined similarly. For example, if and are Dedekind cuts, thenand

item.title: Sequential Circuits
item.description:
Moore Machine: output is a function of only the state, not the input Mealy Machine: output is a function of both the state and inputExcitation equations describe memory (FF or latchlatch) input signals as a function of inputs and current state (i.e., state variables) Transition equations describe the next state as a function of input or current stateConsider the following:Exercise 1. Design a Moore sequential circuit with one input IN and one output OUT, such that OUT is one if and only if IN is 1 for three consecutive clock cycles.
We design the following state machinestate machine
with the following transition table.

We set and because we will be using D FFsD FFs . From the table, we derive (by simply writing the SOPSOP or drawing a Karnaugh MapKarnaugh Map)Our circuit diagram is as follows:
The Verilog implementation is as follows:1module SequenceDetectorV1 (input clk, input in, output out);2 reg [1:0] Q;3 4 // Initial state5 initial Q = 2'b00;6 7 // Next-state logic8 reg [1:0] D;9 10 // Alternatively, we can use assign and wires11 always @*12 begin13 D[1] <= in & (Q[1] | Q[0]);14 D[0] <= in & (Q[1] | ~Q[0]);15 end16 17 // State Update18 always @(posedge clk)19 Q <= D;20 21 // Output logic22 assign out = (Q[1] & Q[0]);23endmodule // SequenceDetectorV1Warning In an always ​ block, do not use = ​ for assignment. Instead, use <= ​. Remark 2. If we were lazy, we could have avoided designing the combinational circuit. Instead, we could have jumped straight to the implementation as follows: 1module SequenceDetectorV2(input clk, input in, output out);2 reg [1:0] Q;3 reg [1:0] D; // Q_Next = D4 5 parameter zero1s = 2'b00;6 parameter one1 = 2'b01;7 parameter two1s = 2'b10;8 parameter three1s = 2'b11;9 10 initial Q = zero1s;11 12 // State Transitions13 always @*14 case(Q)15 zero1s: if (in) D <= one1; else D <= zero1s;16 one1: if (in) D <= two1s; else D <= zero1s;17 two1s: if (in) D <= three1s; else D <= zero1s;18 three1s:if (in) D <= three1s; else D <= zero1s;19 endcase20 21 // State Update22 always @(posedge clk)23 Q <= D;24 25 // Output logic26 assign out = (Q == three1s);27endmodule // SequenceDetectorV2 This is preferred due to its superior readability. Warning When specifying the state transitions, always assign to D ​ in all possible branches. Strange things happen if you forget!

item.title: Two Level Logic Minimization
item.description: Karnaugh Maps are useful.Definition 1 (Implicant). Any product term that implies a function . Definition 2 (Prime Implicant). An implicant such that if one literal is removed from the product, it is no longer an implicant. Definition 3 (Essential Prime Implicant). A prime implicant that covers a minterm not covered by any other prime implicant. The correct ordering along a Karnaugh map is , , , .

item.title: Inductors
item.description: An inductor stores energy in the magnetic field. We relate voltage and current through an inductionwhere is the inductance, measured in Henries (). must be a continuous function of . Remark 1. When there is no voltage across an inductor in DC, an inductor acts as a short circuit. Integrating power as before, we find thatNote that increasing current increases the energy in an inductor. Parallel inductors are combined by taking their inverse of the sum of the inverses of their individual inductance.

item.title: First Order Circuits
item.description:
To solve, we must have the following: 1 initial condition per energy storage element CapacitorCapacitor voltages and inductorinductor currents must be continuous
We use KVLKVL to produce a differential equation and common sense to arrive at its initial conditions. The solution to the resultant homogeneous equationhomogeneous equation is called the natural solution. In addition, the particular solutionparticular solution is called the forced solution.
Represent circuit as Thevenin equivalentThevenin equivalent. By applying KVLKVL, we obtain
where . This is the natural responsenatural response discharging equation.
Represent circuit as Norton equivalentNorton equivalent.

item.title: Time Varying
item.description: The exponential waveform can indicate that something bad is happening if it grows rapidly. A summation of step functions can approximate arbitrary functions.Definition 1 (Natural Response). The solution to a source-free equation is called the natural response. CapacitorsA capacitor stores energy in the electric field. We relate current and voltage across a capacitor aswhere is the capacitance, measured in Farads (). must be a continuous function of . Remark 1. In DC, since is constant, so it acts like an open circuit. Integrating power, we have Capacitors in parallel can be combined by adding their capacitance. Conversely, capacitors in series are combined by taking their inverse of the sum of the inverses of their individual capacitance.We follow the sign convention of current entering the terminal of the capacitor.InductorsAn inductor stores energy in the magnetic field. We relate voltage and current through an inductionwhere is the inductance, measured in Henries (). must be a continuous function of . Remark 1. When there is no voltage across an inductor in DC, an inductor acts as a short circuit. Integrating power as before, we find thatNote that increasing current increases the energy in an inductor. Parallel inductors are combined by taking their inverse of the sum of the inverses of their individual inductance.

item.title: Analysis Techniques
item.description:
Definition 1 (Linear Circuits). A linear circuit is composed entirely of independent sources and linear elements (passive and exhibits a linear - relationship). Resistors, capacitors, inductors, and linear dependent sources are all linear elements. An LED is not a linear element.The analysis techniques discussed apply only to linear circuits.Definition 2 (Supermesh). Supermeshes are formed to avoid current sources in KVL. The current source adds an auxiliary equation relating the 2 mesh currents. Definition 3 (Supernode). Supernodes are formed among nodes between which a voltage source exists (independent or dependent). This allows KCL to be used on the entire supernode and avoids the problem of having an unknown resistance between nodes. If a node inside the supernode is a ground node, it is called a quasi-supernode. The voltage source adds an auxiliary equation relating node voltages. Thevenin and Norton EquivalenceTheorem 1 (Thevenin). A linear circuit can be represented at its output terminals by an equivalent circuit consisting of a series combination of a voltage source and a resistor , where is the open-circuit voltage at those terminals (no load) and is the equivalent resistance between the same terminals when all independent sources in the circuit have been deactivated. is the output resistance of the Thevenin circuit. Theorem 2 (Norton). A linear circuit can be represented at its output terminals by an equivalent circuit consisting of a parallel combination of a current source and a resistor , where . Alternatively, is the short circuit current at the terminals. Deactivate all sources and simplify circuit to a single equivalent resistance.If there are dependent sources, we have two options: We deactivate all independent sources and apply an external voltage source to find . Then, . Use Norton's TheoremNorton's Theorem. We first simplify the circuit by replacing it with a Thevenin equivalent. By voltage division, the load power isMaximum power occurs when , which givesThe circuit is analyzed for each independent source alone and the results are summed.TransistorsThe equivalent circuit is as follows. We assume V. Furthermore, it obeys KCL, so . is referred to as the gain factor.
There is an ideal diode in series with . The - curve consists of 3 regions: Off Saturation (on): increases somewhat linearly. Active (on): plateaus

item.title: Thevenin and Norton Equivalence
item.description: Theorem 1 (Thevenin). A linear circuit can be represented at its output terminals by an equivalent circuit consisting of a series combination of a voltage source and a resistor , where is the open-circuit voltage at those terminals (no load) and is the equivalent resistance between the same terminals when all independent sources in the circuit have been deactivated. is the output resistance of the Thevenin circuit. Theorem 2 (Norton). A linear circuit can be represented at its output terminals by an equivalent circuit consisting of a parallel combination of a current source and a resistor , where . Alternatively, is the short circuit current at the terminals. Deactivate all sources and simplify circuit to a single equivalent resistance.If there are dependent sources, we have two options: We deactivate all independent sources and apply an external voltage source to find . Then, . Use Norton's TheoremNorton's Theorem. We first simplify the circuit by replacing it with a Thevenin equivalent. By voltage division, the load power isMaximum power occurs when , which gives

item.title: Transistors
item.description: The equivalent circuit is as follows. We assume V. Furthermore, it obeys KCL, so . is referred to as the gain factor. There is an ideal diode in series with . The - curve consists of 3 regions: Off Saturation (on): increases somewhat linearly. Active (on): plateaus

item.title: Capacitors
item.description: A capacitor stores energy in the electric field. We relate current and voltage across a capacitor aswhere is the capacitance, measured in Farads (). must be a continuous function of . Remark 1. In DC, since is constant, so it acts like an open circuit. Integrating power, we have Capacitors in parallel can be combined by adding their capacitance. Conversely, capacitors in series are combined by taking their inverse of the sum of the inverses of their individual capacitance.We follow the sign convention of current entering the terminal of the capacitor.

item.title: Heaps
item.description: A tree is a connected graph with no cycles. Alternatively

item.title: Operational Amplifiers
item.description:
An op-amp contains many components internally. Each pin has a specific function: Pin 2: negative input voltage, Pin 3: positive input voltage Pin 4: negative terminal of power supply Pin 7: positive terminal of power supply Pin 6: output voltage,
By KCLKCL, we must have . As long as the op amp is in its linear region, does not affect is operation.Warning Note that !
In the linear region, we have , where is called the open-loop gain. The maximum value that can reach is . We also define the closed loop gain where .
We make the following assumptions to simplify calculations: In reality, they are very small but nonzero (which would result in no amplification) This is because we assume and Note that is connected to .
Changing the gain affects the dynamic range of . Increasing the gain reduces the dynamic range.
Applying our assumptionsassumptions, we haveRemark 1. The input resistance is the Thevenin resistance of the op-amp circuit as seen by . Note that is connected to . There is also an additional resistor .
This is called an inverting amplifier because the gain is negative. We find that Warning Under the ideal op-amp model, it is impossible to compute .
By connecting multiple sources in parallel at terminal , the circuit becomes an adder. In the diagram above haveFor input voltage sources, this also applies. We can create a non-inverting adder by passing the output of the inverting summing amp to an inverting amp.Combine a inverting and non-inverting amplifier.

item.title: Methods for Second Order Differential Equations
item.description: Consider a second-order differential equation of the formDefinition 1 (Linear Differential Equation). A second-order differential equation is linear if has the form . This requires that is linear in and . Then, we can rewrite the equation as If , the equation is homogeneous. For the purpose of notation, we introduce the differential operator:Definition 2 (Differential Operator Notation). Let and be continuous functions on an open interval . We define the differential operator for a twice-differentiable function on as We begin our exploration of second-order linear differential equations by considering the case when . First, consider the case when and are constant. Then,We exploit the properties of by substituting it for :Since is nonzero, we conclude that . This is known as the characteristic equation of this differential equation. We conclude that solutions to the differential equation can be expressed as , where is a root of its characteristic equation.If we have only real roots ( and , distinct), our general solution is . If we have complex roots () and require real solutions, we apply Euler's Formula to arrive at .If we are given a solution , we can find the other solution . We set and substitute into our differential equation. Rearranging, we find that which is a first-order differential equation on . This method tells us that when we have repeated roots, we multiply the second solution by . Intuitively, we restore a degree of freedom that would otherwise be destroyed by the repeated roots, ensuring that we cover all possible solutions with our general solution.What do we do when ?We can write the general solution as , where is the general solution to the corresponding homogeneous equation. This part has no effect on the overall equation as they evaluate to zero when the differential operator is applied. is any particular solution, which we describe how it is found in the following sections.This method applies when and are constants and is a combination of polynomials, exponentials, sines, and cosines. We guess the form of based on :Here is the smallest non-negative integer chosen so that no term in is a solution of the homogeneous equation. Substitute the guess into the ODE, match coefficients, and solve.If is a sum of such terms, find a particular solution for each term separately and add them (by superposition).This method works for any continuous and does not require constant coefficients. Given the homogeneous solutions and , we seek a particular solution of the formWe impose the condition to simplify the derivatives. Substituting into the ODE yields the systemSolving by Cramer's rule giveswhere is the Wronskian. Integrate to find and , then the particular solution isRemark 3 (Comparison). Undetermined coefficients is simpler but limited to constant-coefficient equations with specific forms of . Variation of parameters is fully general but requires computing integrals that may not have closed-form solutions. Theorem 4 (Existence and Uniqueness Theorem for Second Order Linear Differential Equations). Consider the initial value problem subject to and , where , , and are continuous on an open interval that contains . There exists a unique solution over the entire interval . Theorem 5 (Principle of Superposition). If and are solutions of the differential equation , then any linear combination of and is also a solution. The specific linear combination is specifically determined by the initial conditions.Definition 6 (Wronskian). Given two solutions and of a second order linear differential equation, the Wronskian is defined as Definition 7 (Fundamental Set of Solutions). When , and form a fundamental set of solutions, and there exists a unique solution satisfying the initial conditions. This means that the solution space of the differential equation and the space of initial conditions are isomorphic. Theorem 8 (Abel's Theorem). If and are solutions of the second-order linear differential equation where and are continuous on an open interval , then the Wronskian is given by where is a certain constant that depends on and , but not on . Further, either is zero for all in (if ) or else is never zero in (if ).
item.guid: Differential Equations/Methods for Second Order Differential Equations.md

item.title: Sorting
item.description:
Advantages: Stable Advantages: Stable Advantages: Disadvantages: Unstable Advantages: Worst case is In-place with extra memory Disadvantages: Many cache misses Unstable Counts number of a finite set of items and places them accordingly. Count each category Calculate start indices Copy to each element to correct region, incrementing indices This is a stable sort.Advantages: Average time is Efficient memory/cache use! Better than HeapsortHeapsort Disadvantages: Worst case time is Unstable Making Quicksort stable makes it less competitive Partitioning is fragile Performance is very dependent on pivot choice Divide and Conquer approach: Base case: Arrays of length 0 and 1 are already sorted Inductive step: Guess an pivot to partition the array Last element, middle element, median of 3 Place all elements less than to its left and all elements greater than to its right Initialize left pointer to front and right pointer to end Walk forwards and backwards until and such that and do not cross Swap and Repeat until and collide Swap to the correct location Recursively sort elements to the left and to the right of Use tail recursion on the larger side Note that this sort is unstable. Ideally, we would guess to be the median. This would evenly divide the array so that the left and right arrays to are equally sized. However, this is not feasible, so we randomly pick (usually the last element). Note that this algorithm is on sorted arrays. A better partition algorithm is as follows: Pick middle value or median of 3 elements (better) Swap with last element Pivot is set to last element This simplifies the implementation as the pivot is guaranteed to stay in place during the sorting process Worst case (Pivot is always an extreme): Best case (Pivot divides equally): Average case: Requires one non-tail recursive call and one tail recursive call. Depends on pivot choice. Without tail recursion optimization and pivot is bad: or Worst case: Sorting algorithm called over each region of an array depends on certain characteristics
If array is small, use Insertion sortInsertion sort
If Quicksort is recursing too deeply, use HeapsortHeapsort This means we are picking bad pivots
Otherwise, use QuicksortQuicksort "Conquer and combine". Very similar to Quicksort. Sort left half, sort right half, merge. Designed for external sorting. Advantages Stable Use when merging to ensure stable sorting. Disadvantages Never better than Requires additional memory, unlike Quicksort which is in-place Also copies around data Generally slower than Quicksort Out of place merge requires additional memory for merging. We can write an algorithm to merge in-place, but we sacrifice stability and performance. Base case: 0 or 1 items Compute middle () Sort left half Sort right half Merge left and right Begin with base cases and merge segments until entire array is sorted. Always (worst, best, average) Safe for recursion, recursive depth grows slowly Requires additional memory

item.title: Flip Flops and Latches
item.description:
Sequential circuits depend on past states.A finite state machine is defined by two functions:where and are the current and next state variables, are the input variables, and are the output variables.An FSM can be represented in several ways including State transition equation State transition table State transition graph
We require that , which is violated when .
The invalid state can cause bad things, such as the oscillation above.
Note that .
Data only written when clock signal () is high.
Sample inputs only once per clock cycle.Synchronous reset: reset value when clock goes high Asynchronous reset: reset value immediately

item.title: Methods for First Order Differential Equations
item.description:
We consider differential equations of the form Generally, we write a first-order differential equation in the standard form: If a first-order ODE is in a sufficiently nice form, we can employ one of several methods to ascertain its solutions. Consider a first-order differential equation in its standard form. We choose and multiply our equation by , givingNoting that , we haveWhen the integral cannot be evaluated, the general solution is where is some convenient lower bound and is a constant of integration.Sometimes we can separate terms containing from those dependent on . This allows us to write By integrating both sides, we are able to remove the derivative terms and solve for . Remark 1 (Constant solutions). Sometimes it is easy to find a constant solution that satisfies . If we have a differential equation of the form where , , and are continuous functions in a rectangular region such that , then it is an exact differential equation. It follows that there exists a function that satisfies Equations of this form are easily solved by integrating to arrive at written in terms of an unknown function . We then differentiate and solve to determine and consequently, . The final solution is written implicitly as . It is simple to check that differentiating The solutions of the vast majority of first-order initial value problems cannot be found by analytical means. Thus, we are sometimes forced to study the solutions through numerical approximations. One of these is to draw a direction field, but it does not lend itself to quantitative computation. We investigate methods that address this shortcoming.Euler's method is an iterative algorithm that approximates how a function evolves over time based on discrete samples of . At step , we haveTake , so that it satisfies the initial condition of our ODE. Then, we integrateand solve for the constant of integration to satisfy . We continue this process, possibly infinitely, to arrive at a solution. In general,This allows us to generate a sequence . This is useful for proving the existence and uniqueness of solutions. Remark 2 (Picard's Iteration is a contraction mapping.).We would like to know when an equation has a solution given some initial condition and other solutions exist. Theorem 3 (Existence and Uniqueness for First-Order Nonlinear Equations). Let the functions and be continuous in some rectangle defined by and containing the point . Then, in some interval contained in , there is a unique solution of the initial value problem subject to Further, the continuity of implies existence, and the continuity of implies uniqueness. Corollary 4 (Existence and Uniqueness for First-Order Linear Equations). If the functions and are continuous on an open interval containing the point , then there exists a unique function that satisfies the differential equation for each , and that also satisfies the initial condition where is an arbitrary prescribed initial value. Proof. Substituting and , and applying the Existence and Uniqueness Theoremthe Existence and Uniqueness Theorem, we find that the continuity of and are sufficient and necessary for there to exist a unique solution. □Definition 5 (Autonomous Differential Equation). An autonomous differential equation is a first-order differential equation in which the independent variable does not appear explicitly. Such equations have the form
Definition 6 (Critical Points). The zeros of in an Autonomous Differential EquationAutonomous Differential Equation are called critical points. Definition 7 (Asymptotically Stable). An equilibrium point is asymptotically stable if there exists an such that for all , the solutions that pass through approach as . The logistic equation is of the form where is the intrinsic growth rate and is the carrying capacity.We can modify the logistic growth equation to include a threshold as follows:where and . Note that is asymptotically unstable at and asymptotically stable at and .
item.guid: Differential Equations/Methods for First Order Differential Equations.md

item.title: Master Theorem
item.description: Theorem 1 (Master Theorem). Let be a monotonically increasing function that satisfies where and . If , then Furthermore, if for some , then .

item.title: Clock level sensitive d latch
item.guid: Logic Design/Attachments/Clock level sensitive d latch.png

item.title: Timing and Delay
item.description:
There is a delay between when the inputs of a gate change and the output is updated.We denote the propagation delay of a gate from low to high at its output as . Likewise, is the propagation delay from high to low at its output. In general, .The overall circuit delay depends on the transition path taken from the updated input to the updated output. Note that there is no delay if the output is not changed.We systematically analyze the delay by analyzing the effect of only one input at a time and finding the path delays. If multiple paths from an input can change the output, only the latest path has an effect.We can specify the delay of a gate in Verilog as follows:1<gate> #(<rise delay>, <fall delay>) <name>(...);or in behavioral Verilog:1assign #(<rise delay>, <fall delay>) <name> = ...;In both versions, we can use the shorthand notation #<delay> ​. Note that the output of the final not ​ gate is constant.
Note that the output of the final not ​ gate oscillates between high and low. This acts like a clock.
The oscillator is enabled when low and disabled when high.1`timescale 1ns/1ns2module N_Stage_RingOsc();3 reg enable; // When 1, stop; when 0, start4 parameter N = 5; // This is the number of "inverters" and must be odd5 wire [N:1] Waveform;6 7 parameter notDELAY = 2;8 parameter norDELAY = 4;9 10 genvar i;11 generate12 for (i = 1; i < N; i = i + 1)13 begin : notGates14 not #notDELAY notGate(Waveform[i+1], Waveform[i]);15 end16 nor #norDELAY norGate(Waveform[1], Waveform[N], enable);17 endgenerate18 19 initial begin20 enable = 1;21 #(norDELAY+(N-1)*notDELAY); // Run long enough to initialize waveforms22 enable = 0; // Start the oscillation23 end24endmodule

item.title: Combinational Blocks
item.description:
is implemented as . Any -variable function can be implemented using a to decoder. Each one-hot output is a minterm of the inputs, so the desired high outputs are passed into an OR gate. EN is set high.Decoders can be cascaded to create larger decoders, where one decoder is designated to enable one of the other encoders (one-hot), and the remaining inputs are fed directly to each block.
Implementation: The assert bit (), solves the problem that arises from the fact that the inputs are assumed to be one-hot. The block sets high if this condition is met.
If , then is given a higher priority to . The outputs are one-hot, and is set high, where is the largest bit such that is high.A selector/switch. The output copies one of inputs, determined by the selector bits.
The output is set to , where is interpreted as a binary number. The specific implementation is a sum of minterms of , with each AND-ed with an input . Note that each product term is exclusive.Remark 1 (Larger multiplexers). A 16-input mux can be constructed using 4 4-input multiplexers and 1 decoder. The decoder (controlled by 2 selector bits) selects which mux to enable. The remaining 2 selector bits are fed into each of the 4 multiplexers. Each mux is fed 4 of the 16 inputs. Alternatively, we can use 5 multiplexers, all with EN set to high. 4 of the multiplexers are fed 4 input bits. 2 of the selector bits are fed into each mux. The remaining 2 are fed into the final mux. The 4 outputs of the 4 multiplexers are fed into the final mux. Remark 2 (Implementing functions with multiplexers). An variable function can be implemented using an -select ( input) mux. of the function's inputs are fed directly to the selector bits. We set each input corresponding to a high output to high and each input corresponding to a low output to low. The -th variable is fed in as an input (possibly negated) according to the function's minterm expansion.
This is an application of Bool's Expansion TheoremBool's Expansion Theorem.
Opposite of a mux. Connects one input to one of several outputs. Implemented using a decoder, where the input is connected to EN and the selector bits are fed into the decoder's inputs.
Acts as an open circuit when is low and a short circuit when is high. Active-low and inverted variants also exist.

item.title: Boolean Algebra
item.description: Definition 1 (Minterm). A minterm is an AND term of literals. Definition 2 (Maxterm). A maxterm is an OR term of literals. It follows that . Also known as Disjunctive Normal Form Sum of minterms where Notated as Also known as Conjunctive Normal Form Product of maxterms where Notated as Definition 3 (Controlling Inputs). An input that determines the output of a gate regardless of the other inputs. Theorem 4 (Consensus). Definition 5 (Functional Completeness). A set of operations is functionally-complete (or universal) if and only if every switching function can be expressed entirely by means of operations from this set. The following sets are functionally complete: (NAND) Theorem 6 (Boole's Expansion Theorem). A switching function of variables can be decomposed into functions of variables as follows: or . We call the positive cofactor of with respect to . Similarly, the negative cofactor of with respect to .

item.title: Binary Arithmetic
item.description: We have several options for representing signed -bit numbers. Inputs are and . Outputs Inputs are , , and . Outputs Note that an XOR gate for is reused between and to calculate . To create an -bit adder, chain full adders together. We can implement subtraction simply by negating using and adding 1 (2's complement) to . Overflow detection is implemented as .

item.title: Resistive Circuits
item.description: Materials are classified as conductors (primarily metals), semiconductors, or dielectrics (insulators) according to the magnitudes of their conductivities.Definition 1 (Conductivity and Resistivity). The conductivity of a material is a measure of how easily electrons can drift through the material when an external voltage is applied across it. Resistivity () is the inverse () of conductivity. Conductivity is expressed in Siemens per meter (). The Siemen is the inverse of .For a longitudinal resistor, its resistance (given in ) is given bywhere is the length of the material and is its cross-sectional area.Common resistor variations include Thermistor Piezoresistor Light-dependent R (LDR) Rheostat Potentiometer Ohm's law is used to model the - characteristics of a resistor. This relationship is approximately linear in the linear region defined by to .The - relationship iswhere is the reverse saturation current, is the elementary charge, is the temperature in Kelvin, and is the Boltzmann constant.We simplify this model as follows: When the diode is reverse biased, it acts as an open circuit. When the diode is forward biased, it does not conduct unless , where is the forward voltage (or knee voltage). In an ideal diode, . We model this using the equivalent circuit of an ideal diode and voltage source in series. Usually, the voltage source is assumed to be V. Resistors connected in parallel divide the input current among the branches. In particular,Resistors connected in series divide the total voltage across all the resistors. In particular,where is the total resistance of the section where is calculated.

item.title: Containers
item.description: LIFO vector ​ or array ​ deque ​ used in STL Singly-linked list Higher memory overhead FIFO Circular buffer deque ​ used in STL Singly-linked list Higher memory overhead Double-ended queue, supports random access Circular buffer Doubly-linked list Singly-linked lists do not support efficient removal Supports insertion, with removal in descending priority Heap Sorted sequence Array of linked lists Only if priorities are small integers
